SECTION 1.  
PROJECT CONSIDERATIONS 
The proposed project site is located off 3rd Street to the northwest of the existing 
Dry Dock 3 of the Pearl Harbor Naval Shipyard and Intermediate Maintenance Facility (PHNSY & 
IMF) in Pearl Harbor on the Island of Oahu, Hawaii. The project location and general vicinity are 
shown on the Project Location Map, Plate 1. 
The proposed new dry dock facility will be located in the Controlled Industrial Area (CIA) 
between Dry Dock 3 and the abandoned Marine Railway No. 2.  It is envisioned that the dry dock 
will be a concrete gravity structure approximately 650 feet long and 100 feet wide with a depth of 
about 55 feet.  Fill will be required to establish grade given that much of the area surrounding the 
dry dock is currently in water.  The areas surrounding the dry dock will be paved to support vehicular 
traffic and production shop spaces. 
A design-level field exploration and laboratory testing program consisting of both land 
and water borings was undertaken to assist the design team in the preliminary design of the 
project. This geotechnical data report specifically addresses the field exploration and laboratory 
testing program for the land boring work only.  A geotechnical data report for the water boring 
work has been transmitted under separate cover. 
A summary of the field exploration program for the land borings conducted for the project 
is presented below. Detailed information and discussions for each specific work item are 
provided in the respective appendices of this geotechnical data report. The surveyed as-drilled 
investigation locations are shown on the Boring Location Plan, Plate 2.

SECTION 2.  
SCOPE OF WORK 
The purpose of our field exploration and laboratory testing program is to obtain an 
overview of the surface and subsurface conditions at the Dry Dock 3 Replacement site in support 
of the design of the new dry dock facility project. 
Our work was performed in accordance with the scope of services outlined in our revised 
fee proposal dated September 24, 2019, and the Technical Services Subcontract entered into on 
March 11, 2020.  In order to accomplish this, we conducted a field exploration and laboratory 
testing program consisting of the following tasks and efforts: 
1. 
Research and review of in-house geologic and soils information in the project 
vicinity. A list of the references used in the preparation of this geotechnical data 
report is presented in the reference section. 
2. 
Procurement of base passes for our field personnel and subcontractors for access 
to the project site, including DBIDS for JBPHH and CIA badges for PHNSY & IMF. 
3. 
Preparation and submittal of the following project documents in support of our 
field exploration activities and briefing of our field personnel of the plans: 
Geotechnical Investigation Work Plan 
Accident Prevention Plan with Activity Hazard Analysis 
4. 
Site reconnaissance to review the boring locations and access considerations for 
drilling. 
5. 
Surveying of the proposed and as-drilled boring locations by our subcontractor, 
ControlPoint Surveying, Inc. 
6. 
Toning of underground utilities by our subcontractor, GeoTek Hawaii, Inc. and 
initiation of Hawaii One Call process for utility clearance by our                           
field engineer/geologist. 
7. 
Provision of safety devices around the work area and safety flags for vehicles. 
8. 
Provision of a dedicated Site Safety and Health Officer (SSHO) during our field 
exploration work. 
9. 
Performance of energy measurement of standard penetration test (SPT) 
automatic trip hammer system.

10. 
Mobilization and demobilization of a truck-mounted drill rig, water truck, and

Drilling and sampling of five land borings extending to depths of about 201.5 and 
202 feet below the existing pavement surface for a total of about 1,009.5 lineal 
feet of exploration.

Backfilling of the drilled borings with cement grout upon completion of the soil 
and/or rock sampling program, with the exception of the seismic shear wave

engineer/geologist.

a designated area of the project site by our office for subsequent testing and 
disposal of the investigation derived wastes (IDW) by our subcontractor, Element 
Environmental, LLC.

our field exploration as an aid in classifying the materials and evaluating their 
engineering properties.

geotechnical data review, subsurface explorations, groundwater observations,

20. 
Quality assurance of our work on the project by our principal engineer.

Miscellaneous work efforts such as drafting, word processing, and clerical support.

SECTION 3.  
SITE CHARACTERIZATION 
3.1 
Regional Geology 
The Island of Oahu was built by the extrusion of basaltic lavas from two shield volcanoes, 
Waianae and Koolau. The older volcano, Waianae, is estimated to be middle to late Pliocene in 
age and forms the bulk of the western one-third of the island. The younger shield, Koolau, is 
estimated to be late Pliocene to early Pleistocene (Ice Age) in age and forms the majority of the 
eastern two-thirds of the island. The Koolau shield reached the end of its main shield-building 
phase about 2 million years ago. 
The geologic conditions underlying the project site are the result of a complex series of 
deposition and erosional events brought on by glacio-eustatic sea level changes and 
post-erosional volcanic activities during the Pleistocene Epoch. Following the cessation of 
volcanic activity of the Koolau Volcano, alluvial soil materials were deposited in a deltaic or 
estuarine environment in the area of Pearl Harbor. 
The sea level fluctuated several times, receding perhaps 300 feet below and rising about 
100 feet above the present sea level. Concurrent with a rise in sea level during the late 
Pleistocene era, eruption of the Salt Lake-Makalapa member of the Honolulu Volcanic Series 
mantled the older alluvium with thick deposits of volcanic ash, which consolidated to form 
volcanic tuff (locally known as “mudrock”) layers.  Subsequent erosion dissected and thinned 
those mantles of volcanic tuff.  
During the recent epoch, as sea level rose to its current stand, the area was covered with 
soft and loose lagoonal marine deposits. These deposits were subsequently covered with 
man-made fills placed during the middle portion of this century for land reclamation projects 
during construction of the existing Pearl Harbor Naval Shipyard.  The waterfront facility project 
site is located at the edge of the former Pearl Harbor shoreline. The subsurface conditions of the 
project site generally consist of fill materials overlying coralline deposits and highly weathered 
volcanic tuff materials.   
SECTION 3. SITE CHARACTERIZATION  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 6 
Hawaii  California 
3.2 
Subsurface Characteristics 
In general, we observed four main stratigraphic units across the project area within the 
proposed dry dock waterfront facility. The stratigraphic units consist of soil and rock materials of 
varying character and engineering properties. The four main stratigraphic units are listed below 
in the general order of geologic age, beginning with the younger unit and ending with the older 
unit: 
1. 
Recent Fill (Rf) 
2. 
Quaternary Honolulu Tuffs (Qht) 
3. 
Coral Limestone (Qls) 
4. 
Older Alluvium (Qa) 
It should be noted that the surface fills are generally underlain by interbedded layers of 
volcanic tuffs, coralline detritus, coral/sandstone formations, and older alluvium.  The following 
sections briefly describe the general characteristics of each of the above stratigraphic units based 
on our literature research, experience with similar materials, and the field exploration and 
laboratory testing program conducted at selected locations across the project site. The 
approximate delineation of these stratigraphic units at the ground surface of the project vicinity 
is presented on the Geology Map, Plate 3. 
3.2.1 Recent Fill (Rf) 
This stratigraphic unit represents low-lying areas that were filled during urbanization. The 
majority of these low-lying areas are underlain at relatively shallow depths by recent 
alluvium and were originally marsh areas. Small parts of the filled lands are underlain by 
older alluvial soil deposits. In areas underlain by the recent alluvium, the fill materials 
generally consist of elastic silts and sandy silts with stiff to hard consistencies. 
The quality and composition of the fill materials are variable. These fills were frequently 
placed without proper compaction control. Therefore, it is difficult to make any general 
statement regarding their engineering properties or construction in these areas. 
3.2.2 Quaternary Honolulu Tuffs (Qht) 
Tuff from vents of the Honolulu Volcanic Series consists of volcanic ash, which 
consolidated or hardened to form a medium hard rock that resembles siltstone or 
SECTION 3. SITE CHARACTERIZATION  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 7 
Hawaii  California 
sandstone, locally called “mudrock”. The tuff is frequently found overlying coralline 
deposits or older alluvium units. This formation has relatively low permeability, and 
groundwater generally is not encountered in this formation. When it does occur, it is 
usually in the form of seepage along joints. 
3.2.3 Coral Limestone (Qls) 
This unit consists of fossil remnants of coral-algal reefs and their related detrital debris 
deposits. In Hawaii, the detrital deposits tend to be the dominant portion of any coralline 
rock assemblage. The detrital deposits are weakly to strongly cemented coralline debris 
consisting of gravel, sand, and silt-sized clastic fragments. Some of the coralline fragments 
were dissolved, and the solution cavities were filled with cemented and/or uncemented 
fine sands and silts. The following subunits are used in this geotechnical data report. 
Coralline Detritus 
This subunit consists of granular detritus broken from reef formations. The soils 
generally consist of medium dense to dense poorly graded sands and gravel. Silts 
and clays are only present in minor or trace amounts. Detritus also may be weakly 
cemented with occasional moderate to strong cementation. 
Coral Formation 
This subunit is composed of coral and calcareous algae in their original growth 
positions or detrital deposits, which have become moderately to well cemented. 
Sandstone 
Sandstone is a sedimentary rock composed mainly of cemented sand-sized 
particles (clasts) consisting of individual mineral particles or grains of the 
weathered parent rock. The sedimentary particles comprising sandstone typically 
are derived from the breakdown of parent material into sedimentary particles. 
The sedimentary particles typically are eroded, transported and sorted by water 
or air, and deposited as layers of sedimentary materials. Over time, the sediments 
may be buried by other sediments or geologic layers, and the burial pressure 
combined with a cementing agent act to consolidate the grains and cement the 
SECTION 3. SITE CHARACTERIZATION  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 8 
Hawaii  California 
pore space between the grains to form hardened sandstone. Typical cementing 
agents may be calcium carbonate (common in calcareous sand deposits of Hawaii) 
or silica and some clays.  
Most sandstones in Hawaii are light brown in color and calcareous being derived 
from the breakdown of parent coralline and reef materials into sand-sized 
particles. The cementing agent in most calcareous sandstone is calcium carbonate. 
Inclusions of basaltic rock fragments, marine shells, and other fragments of broken 
coral may be cemented in the sandstone layers. Sandstones containing such clast 
inclusions commonly are referred to as beachrock in Hawaii, having been derived 
from available shoreline geologic materials.  
The strength (hardness) of sandstone generally depends on the grain size, degree 
of sorting, and degree of cementation of the sedimentary materials. The 
weathering and decomposition of calcareous sandstone may be accelerated by 
exposure to acidic water, which attacks the calcareous cement binding the sand 
grains and causes a slow exfoliation of the outermost exposed layers of the 
sandstone outcropping. Sandstone may or may not contain visible bedding 
surfaces. Aeolian (wind derived) sandstone may exhibit cross-bedding structures 
that developed during the initial deposition of the sand layers. 
3.2.4 Older Alluvium (Qa) 
Older alluvial deposits are generally very stiff to hard silty and clayey soils that originally 
were deposited in alluvial environments, buried over long periods of time by more recent 
deposits to become consolidated, and then subsequently uncovered at the ground 
surface by erosion or other geologic processes. This unit generally overlies the Koolau 
Basalt and consists of terrigenous sediments, which have been transported by stream 
action from the upper reaches of the streams.  
Gravel lenses representing buried stream channels are sometimes encountered. Portions 
of the Older Alluvium unit contain some weathered basaltic cobbles and boulders. These 
coarse-grained parts of the unit also may contain artesian groundwater from the basal 
SECTION 3. SITE CHARACTERIZATION  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 9 
Hawaii  California 
freshwater system or as a sub-aquifer. The deposits may resemble saprolite soil deposits, 
but they are clearly differentiable by the layered characteristics. Frequently, these 
materials show a moderate to high shrink/swell potential when subjected to fluctuations 
in moisture. The following subunits are used in this geotechnical data report. 
Older Alluvium 
This subunit generally consists of tan to brown fat clays with varying amounts of 
sands and infrequent pockets of gravel and cobbles. The consistency is generally 
in the range of very stiff to hard.  
Old Lagoonal Deposit 
This subunit generally consists of light gray to medium gray sandy silts or silty 
sands in stiff to very stiff consistency and/or medium dense to dense relative 
density with various degrees of cementation. In some cases, strongly cemented 
deposits form siltstones. Gravel-sized fragments are rare. Frequently, the silts are 
organic in origin and may have a mild to strong organic odor.  
3.2.5 Groundwater 
Groundwater level measurements were taken during the advancement of the boreholes 
drilled for the project during our field exploration program. We encountered 
groundwater level in the drilled borings at depths ranging from about 4 to 7.8 feet below 
the existing pavement surface. The groundwater levels measured generally correspond 
to about Elevations -0.62 to +3.1 feet Mean Sea Level (MSL). It should be noted that 
groundwater levels are subject to change due to tidal fluctuations, storm surge conditions, 
rainfall, time of year, seasonal precipitation, surface water runoff, and other factors. 
We explored the subsurface conditions across the project site by drilling and sampling 
five borings, designated as Boring Nos. DD5-2A, DD5-3, DD5-4, DD5-5, and DD5-6, extending to 
depths of about 201.5 and 202 feet below the existing pavement surface.  Three bulk samples of 
the near-surface soils, designated as Bulk-1 through Bulk-3, were obtained to evaluate the 
moisture-density relationships of the near-surface soils. The approximate boring and bulk sample 
locations are shown on the Boring Location Plan, Plate 2. 
SECTION 3. SITE CHARACTERIZATION  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 10 
Hawaii  California 
In general, our field exploration generally encountered 4 to 5 inches of asphaltic concrete 
underlain by fill materials consisting of medium dense poorly graded sands and gravel and stiff 
to very stiff elastic silts and fat clays extending to depths of about 2 to 9 feet below the existing 
pavement surface.  The surface fill materials were underlain by interbedded layers of coralline 
detritus consisting of loose to dense coralline sands and gravel, medium hard to hard coral, 
sandstone, and volcanic tuff formations, and older alluvial soil deposits consisting of stiff to hard 
fat clays and elastic silts, extending to the maximum depth explored of about 202 feet below the 
existing pavement surface. 
Detailed descriptions of our field exploration methodology and the Logs of Borings are 
presented in Appendix A. Results of the laboratory tests performed on selected soil samples and 
rock cores are presented in Appendix C. Photographs of the core samples retrieved from our field 
exploration are presented in Appendix D.

SECTION 4.  
GEOTECHNICAL EXPLORATION PROGRAM 
The geotechnical exploration program conducted for the Dry Dock 3 Replacement project 
generally consisted of the following field and laboratory work components. A brief description of 
each work component is presented below. Detailed discussions, findings, and test results of the 
various components are provided in the respective appendices herein. 
4.1 
Soil Borings, Rock Coring, and Associated Field Testing 
Soil and rock sampling methods and field testing were performed in accordance with the 
Geotechnical Investigation Work Plan dated April 8, 2020, and the Engineering Specification for 
Subsurface Investigation and Laboratory Testing (Rev. 002) dated February 4, 2020 by Bechtel 
National, Inc. The field exploration is described in more detail in the introduction to Appendix A. 
The following discussion provides an overview of the methods and procedures. 
Typically, soil borings were advanced with 4-inch diameter continuous-flight solid-stem 
augers (SSA) to the first encounter with groundwater and then continued with PQ-size wireline 
coring methods to advance the PQ-size casing from the ground surface. In general, auger drilling 
was used in areas where stiff to hard cohesive soils (older alluvium) exist and in locations where 
rock coring was not anticipated. Additionally, SSA drilling was switched over to the wireline coring 
method when hard clays, boulders, and/or rock formation were encountered or when soft and/or 
loose soils that caved in were encountered. 
Sampling of the soils was performed using AWJ drill rods (1.75 OD/1.25 ID) at about 
5 to 10-foot intervals. Drive samples were obtained using the modified California sampler 
(ASTM D3550) and the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) split-spoon sampler (ASTM D1586). 
Sampling of the soft soil formation was performed by pushing a 3-inch OD thin-walled stainless 
steel Shelby tube into the soil formation (ASTM D1587). 
Where the borehole was advanced utilizing the PQ core barrel in the soil formations, 
samples of the soils recovered from the PQ core barrel also were retained in core boxes for visual 
observation.  For intervals where the borehole was advanced utilizing the PQ casing only as a 
rotary wash drilling method, there were no soil samples collected. The borings were advanced 
SECTION 4. GEOTECHNICAL EXPLORATION PROGRAM  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 12 
Hawaii  California 
into the rock formations with the PQ-size wireline coring methods. The PQ-size wireline coring 
methods produce rock core samples of 3.35 inches (85 mm) in diameter. 
In-situ testing of the subsurface materials in the borings included the SPT, which was 
performed in accordance with ASTM D1586. Standard Penetration Test (SPT) sampling provides 
a measure of the resistance or consistency of a soil and is reported as the uncorrected N-value, 
which is defined as the number of blows required to drive a standard split-spoon sampler 
12 inches with a 140-lb. hammer, after 6 inches of initial seating. The penetration resistance of 
the Modified California Sampler was recorded in a similar manner, and the Modified California 
Sampler blows per 12 inches can be approximately related to the SPT N-value through 
correlation. Blow count correlation between the Modified California Sampler and the Standard 
Penetration Test Sampler is presented in Appendix F. 
Rock core recovery (REC) and rock quality designation (RQD) were measured as described 
in Appendix A. ASTM D6032 - Standard Test Method for Determining Rock Quality Designation 
(RQD) of Rock Core states that “Core sizes from BQ to PQ with core diameters of 36.5 mm 
(1.44 in.) to 85 mm (3.35 in.), respectively, are normally acceptable for measuring RQD as long as 
proper drilling techniques are used that do not cause excessive core breakage or poor recovery, 
or both. The NX-size (54.7 mm [2.16 in.]) and NQ-size (47.5 mm [1.87 in.]) are the optimal core 
sizes for measuring RQD. The RQD is also useful for large core diameters provided the core 
diameter is clearly stated.” The rock core photographs are included in Appendix D for information 
and reference. 
4.2 
Seismic Shear Wave Velocity Testing 
Seismic shear wave velocity profiling was performed at a completed borehole 
(Boring No. DD5-2A) using seismic cone penetration testing (SCPT) equipment. Procedures for 
the test are discussed in more detail in Appendix B. In brief, the completed borehole was tremie-
filled with time-release bentonite pellets and bentonite chips in order to provide a medium 
through which the seismic cone tip could be pushed. The sleeve and tip resistance through the 
bentonite-filled borehole is not meaningful because the cone is pushed through the bentonite 
SECTION 4. GEOTECHNICAL EXPLORATION PROGRAM  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 13 
Hawaii  California 
backfill; however, the seismic wave travels from the ground surface through the surrounding 
soils, allowing measurement of the average seismic shear wave velocity. 
The seismic cone tip contains a geophone sensor. At a succession of depths, the 
advancement of the cone tip was halted, a seismic pulse was generated at the ground surface by 
striking a loaded plank with a switched hammer, and the resulting seismic shear wave was 
detected at the geophone. The distance from the source and the wave arrival time allow for 
determination of the average shear wave velocity over a depth interval. The measured average 
seismic shear wave velocity logs are presented in Appendix B. 
4.3 
Geotechnical Laboratory Testing 
Geotechnical laboratory testing was performed on soil and rock samples to determine the 
physical and engineering properties. Laboratory tests performed included moisture content, unit 
weight, Atterberg Limits, grain size distribution, specific gravity, unconfined compression of soil, 
uniaxial compressive strength of rock, Consolidated-Undrained (CU) Triaxial Compression, 
Unconsolidated-Undrained (UU) Triaxial Compression, Consolidation, Modified Proctor, and 
corrosion potential tests (pH, minimum resistivity, chloride content, and sulfate content). The 
laboratory test results are provided in Appendix C. 
4.4 
Environmental Laboratory Testing 
Environmental laboratory testing was performed on the Investigation Derived Wastes 
(IDW) by our subcontractor, Element Environmental, LLC.  A total of ten 55-gallon drums was 
generated for the IDW from the land boring work.  The results of the environmental screening 
and laboratory testing are provided in Appendix G for ease of reference. 
END OF GEOTECHNICAL EXPLORATION PROGRAM 
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
Page 14 
Hawaii  California 
SECTION 5.  
LIMITATIONS 
The geotechnical field and laboratory test data submitted in this geotechnical data report 
are based upon information obtained from the field exploration points. The coordinates and 
elevations of the as-drilled boring locations indicated herein were surveyed by ControlPoint 
Surveying, Inc. and transmitted to our office on May 20, 2020. 
The stratification lines shown on the graphic representations of the field exploration 
points depict the approximate boundaries between soil and rock types and, as such, may denote 
a gradual transition. Water level data presented on the logs of borings in this report were based 
on measurements recorded in the borings at the times shown on the graphic representations. It 
should be noted that fluctuation of the water level may occur due to variation in tides, rainfall, 
temperature, and other factors. 
This report has been prepared for the exclusive use of Bechtel National, Inc. and their 
client, U.S. Department of the Navy, for specific application to the Land Boring Exploration, 
PHNSY Dry Dock 3 Replacement Project, Pearl Harbor Naval Shipyard & IMF, JBPHH, Hawaii in 
accordance with generally accepted geotechnical engineering principles and practices. No 
warranty is expressed or implied.  
This geotechnical data report was not intended to investigate the potential presence of 
hazardous materials existing at the project site. It should be noted that the equipment, 
techniques, and personnel used to conduct a geo-environmental exploration differ substantially 
from those applied in geotechnical engineering. 
END OF LIMITATIONS

GEOLABS, INC.

Hawaii  California

APPENDIX A  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page A-1 
Hawaii  California 
A P P E N D I X   A 
Test Boring Exploration 
We explored the subsurface conditions at selected locations across the project site by 
drilling and sampling five land borings, designated as Boring Nos. DD5-2A, DD5-3, DD5-4, DD5-5, 
and DD5-6. The borings extended to depths of about 201.5 and 202 feet below the existing 
pavement surface using a truck-mounted drill rig equipped with continuous-flight augers and 
rotary coring tools. The field exploration was conducted from April 7 through May 8, 2020. In 
addition, three bulk samples of the near-surface soils, designated as Bulk-1 through Bulk-3, were 
obtained for laboratory testing.  The approximate boring and bulk sample locations are shown 
on the Boring Location Plan, Plate 2. 
Soil borings were advanced with 4-inch diameter continuous-flight solid-stem augers 
(SSA) to the first encounter with groundwater and then continued with PQ-size wireline coring 
methods to advance the PQ-size casing from the ground surface. In general, auger drilling was 
used in areas where stiff to hard cohesive soils (older alluvium) exist and in locations where rock 
coring is not anticipated. Additionally, SSA drilling was switched over to the wireline coring 
method when hard clays, boulders, and/or rock formation were encountered or when soft and/or 
loose soils that caved in were encountered.   After the borings were advanced to the maximum 
depth of the boreholes, the bored holes generally were backfilled with cement-grout with the 
exception of Boring No. DD5-2A. 
Boring No. DD5-2A, selected for seismic shear wave velocity testing, was backfilled with 
0.25-inch diameter time-release bentonite pellets (below the groundwater level) and bentonite 
chips (above the groundwater level). The temporary casing from the coring operations was used 
as a tremie pipe to place the time-release bentonite pellets starting from the bottom and 
advancing upward. Backfilling above the static groundwater table were performed in stages by 
adding water to limited amounts of bentonite chips to ensure hydration before additional 
bentonite chips were placed in the borehole. The top 3 feet of the borehole was then grouted 
with Quikcrete concrete mix. No additives were added to the drilling water during our drilling 
operations. 
Our engineer/geologist classified the materials encountered in the borings by visual and 
textural examination in the field in general accordance with ASTM D2488, Standard Practice for 
Description and Identification of Soils, and monitored the drilling operations on a 
near-continuous basis. These classifications were further reviewed visually and by testing in the 
laboratory. Soils were classified in general accordance with ASTM D2487, Standard Practice for 
Classification of Soils for Engineering Purposes (Unified Soil Classification System), as shown on 
the Soil Log Legend, Plate A-0.1. Deviations made to the soil classification in accordance with 
ASTM D2487 are described on the Soil Classification Log Key, Plate A-0.2. Graphic representations 
of the materials encountered and other observations, such as groundwater levels, circulation 
water condition, drilling rate, etc. are presented on the Logs of Borings, Plates A-1.1 through 
A-5.6. 
Appendix A 
Test Boring Exploration 
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page A-2 
Hawaii • California 
Relatively “undisturbed” soil samples were obtained from the borings drilled in general 
accordance with ASTM D3550, Standard Practice for Thick Wall, Ring-Lined, Spilt Barrel, Drive 
Sampling of Soils, by driving a 3-inch OD modified California sampler with a 140-pound hammer 
falling 30 inches. In addition, some samples were obtained from the borings drilled in general 
accordance with ASTM D1586, Standard Test Method for Penetration Test and Split-Barrel 
Sampling of Soils, by driving a 2-inch OD standard penetration sampler using the same hammer 
and drop. The blow counts needed to drive the sampler the second and third 6 inches of an 
18-inch drive are shown as the “Penetration Resistance” on the Logs of Borings at the appropriate 
sample depths.  Relatively “undisturbed” samples of the soft soil formation were obtained by 
pushing a 3-inch OD thin-walled stainless steel Shelby tube into the soil formation in general 
accordance with ASTM D1587, Standard Practice for Thin-Walled Tube Sampling of Soils for 
Geotechnical Purposes. The range of hydraulic pressures needed to advance the sampler through 
a 24-inch push is shown as the “Penetration Resistance” on the Logs of Borings at the appropriate 
sample depths. 
Pocket penetrometer tests were performed on selected cohesive soil samples retrieved 
in the field. The pocket penetrometer test provides an indication of the unconfined compressive 
strength of the sample. Pocket penetrometer test results are summarized on the Logs of Borings 
at the appropriate sample depths. 
Core samples of the rock materials encountered at the project site were obtained by using 
diamond core drilling techniques, consisting of a PQ wireline coring system with double tube core 
barrel (core size of 3.35 inches), in general accordance with ASTM D2113, Diamond Core Drilling 
for Site Investigation. Core drilling is a rotary drilling method that uses a hollow bit to cut into the 
rock formation. The rock material left in the hollow core of the bit is mechanically recovered for 
examination and description. The coring rates indicated on the Logs of Borings refer to the time 
required to complete that particular core run at that depth. Rock cores were described in general 
accordance with the Rock Description System, as shown on the Rock Log Legend, Plate A-0.3. The 
Rock Description System is based generally on the publication “Suggested Methods for the 
Quantitative Description of Discontinuities in Rock Masses” by the International Society for Rock 
Mechanics (March 1977). 
Recovery (REC) is defined as the actual length of material recovered from a coring attempt 
versus the length of the core attempt. For example, if 3.7 feet of material is recovered from a 
5.0-foot core run, the recovery would be 74 percent and would be shown on the Logs of Borings 
as REC = 74%. 
The Rock Quality Designation (RQD) is also a subjective guide to the relative quality of 
rock masses. RQD is defined as the percentage of the total core run in rock that is sound material 
in excess of 4 inches in length without any discontinuities, discounting any drilling, mechanical, 
and handling induced fractures or breaks. If 2.5 feet of sound material is recovered from a 
5.0-foot core run in rock, the RQD would be 50 percent and would be shown on the Logs of 
Borings as RQD = 50%. Generally, the following is used to describe the relative quality of the rock

Rock Log Legend
The following terms describe the resistance of a rock to indentation or scratching:
Specimen breaks with difficulty after several "pinging" hammer blows.
Example: Dense, fine grain volcanic rock
Specimen breaks with some difficulty after several hammer blows.
Example: Vesicular, vugular, coarse-grained rock
Specimen can be broked by one hammer blow. Cannot be scraped by knife. SPT may penetrate by
~25 blows per inch with bounce.
Example: Porous rock such as clinker, cinder, and coral reef
Can be indented by one hammer blow. Can be scraped or peeled by knife. SPT can penetrate by
~100 blows per foot.
Example: Weathered rock, chalk-like coral reef
Crumbles under hammer blow. Can be peeled and carved by knife. Can be indented by finger
pressure.
Example: Saprolite

APPENDIX B  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page B-1 
Hawaii • California 
A P P E N D I X   B 
Seismic Shear Wave Velocity Test 
Seismic shear wave velocity profiling of the subsurface materials was performed using 
Seismic Cone Penetration Testing (SCPT) equipment. Seismic shear wave velocity testing using 
seismic cone penetration testing equipment was performed at a selected boring location, 
designated as Boring No. DD5-2A(SW), as shown on the Boring Location Plan, Plate 2. 
The seismic shear wave velocity profiling was performed at approximately 3.3 to 6.6-foot 
intervals extending to a depth of about 200 feet below the existing pavement surface. In order 
to conduct the seismic shear wave velocity test in the boring, the test boring generally was 
advanced utilizing rotary coring methods to the maximum depth of the boring. Logs of the 
materials encountered in the boring are presented on the Logs of Boring in Appendix A. After the 
boring was advanced to the maximum depth of the borehole, the bored hole was backfilled with 
0.25-inch diameter time-release bentonite pellets. The temporary casing from the coring 
operations was used as a tremie pipe to place the bentonite pellets starting from the bottom and 
advancing upward. When the bentonite pellets are in contact with the groundwater in the 
borehole, the pellets started to hydrate slowly. As the bentonite pellets hydrate, they swell and 
soften. Backfilling above the static groundwater table was performed in stages by adding water 
to a limited amount of bentonite chips to ensure hydration before additional bentonite chips 
were placed in the borehole. The probe was then pushed through the softened bentonite 
extending to a depth of about 200 feet below the existing pavement surface using the seismic 
cone penetration testing equipment (SCPT). 
The seismic shear wave velocity test consists of hydraulically pushing a 10-ton steel 
electronic subtraction cone into the bentonite backfilled borehole. A catalog cut of the 10-ton 
electronic subtraction cone and the manufacturer calibration report are presented in 
Figures B-1.1 and B-1.2 and Figures B-2.1 through B-2.4 for illustration/information purposes 
only.  The cone has an apex angle of 60 degrees and a projected surface area of 1.55 square 
inches (10 square centimeters) equipped with an electronic depth sensor attached to the cone 
rod. The seismic cone carries a uniaxial horizontal geophone to detect the arrival of a shear wave 
generated and propagated from the ground surface. Seismic measurements are made when the 
SCPT has stopped and a shear wave is sent into the subsurface. A shear wave is generated at the 
surface by striking a loaded plank with a switched hammer. The propagation time of the wave 
from the hammer blow to the cone was measured at each discrete depth interval. The vector 
difference of these depths divided by the time difference for the shear wave to arrive at the 
various depths provided the average shear wave velocity over the depth interval. 
The seismic shear wave velocities measured for the full depth, and the average seismic 
shear wave velocity calculated for the top 100 feet of the soil profile at the boring location are 
presented on Plates B-1.1 through B-1.8. The weighted average seismic shear wave velocity for 
the upper 100 feet of the soil profile was calculated based on the average shear wave velocity  
Appendix B 
Seismic Shear Wave Velocity Test 
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page B-2 
Hawaii  California 
method described in Chapter 20 of the American Society of Civil Engineers (ASCE) 7-16, Minimum 
Design Loads for Buildings and Other Structures. 
Vertek - Hogentogler Cone Penetrometer Products
Please Contact Us at (802) 728-4588 or visit our website at www.vertek.ara.com
Cones & Data Acquisition Systems
10 & 15 Ton Digital Cones for E4FCS
Temperature Compensated - The digital electronic subtraction 10 and 15 
ton cones have been developed to address the accuracy, sensitivity and dura-
bility problems inherent in other cone designs, with a resolution of 6.5 and 10 
Ibs. respectively. The unit consists of a single element strain gauge transducer, 
eliminating mechanical coupling effects, and with cone support electron-
ics packaged directly behind the transducer. Both the cone tip, local friction 
element and pore pressure element are ﬁ eld replaceable by the operator in 
approximately ﬁ ve minutes. The cones are conﬁ gured with one through ﬁ ve 
channel capacity with six channels from which to choose.
Cone Channels:
• Tip
• Local Friction
• Pore Pressure
• Temperature
• Inclination
• Seismic
• Resistivity Module can be added
There are two possible locations for pore pressure: on the tip or directly be-
hind the tip.
The electronics have precision power supplies for the strain gauges, which 
eliminate the effects of cable resistance on the measurements. Subtraction is 
performed digitally by the CPU and calibration data for tip, local friction and 
pore pressure channels is stored in CPU non-volatile memory.  Digital cone 
calibration is capable of correcting for non-linearity and hysterisis in a way 
that analog circuitry cannot. Digital cones conform to all international stan-
dards.
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
FIGURE B-1.1 
Vertek - Hogentogler Cone Penetrometer Products
Please Contact Us at (802) 728-4588 or visit our website at www.vertek.ara.com
Cones & Data Acquisition Systems
Electrical Speciﬁ cations
- Power: +12 Volt at 200 mA (max)
- Connector: 10 pin LEMO waterproof connector
- Operating temperature range: 0oC to 60oC
- Storage temperature: -40oC to 70oC
- 4 channel, 24 bit A/D converter for measurement 
of tip, friction, and pore pressure.  Minimum 
noise free resolution of 1 part in 65000
- 10 channel, 12 bit A/D converter for measure
ment of inclination, seismic, temperature and 
spare channels
- 8 bit microcontroller with 8kb ﬂ ash memory for 
cone identiﬁ cation and calibration data storage
- 32kb non-volatile ram for data storage
- RS-485 serial communication.  Cable lengths of 
1000 ft possible.  CRC error checking to prevent 
transmission of incorrect data
- Cones are interchangeable in the ﬁ eld.  Cones 
can be calibrated in the ﬁ eld.  All calibration data 
is contained in the cone
- Compatible with vision cone and fuel ﬂ uorescence
detector
Mechanical Speciﬁ cations  (10 Ton / 15 Ton)
Tip
- Tip angle: 60 degree
- Tip area: 10 cm2  / 15 cm2
- Net area ratio: 0.8
- Tip range: 100 Mpa (1044 TSF) / 150 Mpa (1566 
TSF)
- Tip mechanical overload capacity: 300 %
- Accuracy: 0.2 %
- Temperature compensated
Sleeve
- Sleeve area: 225 cm2
- Sleeve capacity: 1 MPa
- Sleeve mechanical overload capacity: 200 %
- Accuracy:  0.2 %
- Temperature compensated
- Equal end area eliminates pore pressure 
effects
Pore Pressure Transducer
- Pore pressure range: 3500 kPa (500 psi)
- Pore pressure mechanical overload pres
sure: greater than 200 % 
- Accuracy: 0.5 %
- Temperature compensated
- Special cones available with three pressure 
transducers for simultaneous measurement 
of U1, U2, U3
Inclinometer
- Two channel inclinometer.  Pitch and roll 
measurement provides information on cone 
migration
- Uses accelerometers to measure inclina-
   tion.  No moving parts
- Range: 0 – 15 degrees
- Resolution: 0.1 degree
Seismic
- Uni or Biaxial geophone
- Capacity: +/- 2g
- True DC response
- Electronic offset eliminates effect of gravity.
- Digitized in cone and transmitted over se-
   rial port  No extra wires or analog mea-
   surement required

PLATE B-1.8
APPENDIX C  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page C-1 
Hawaii  California 
A P P E N D I X   C 
Geotechnical Laboratory Tests 
Samples recovered from our field exploration program performed for the project were 
visually examined and classified in our laboratory in Honolulu, Hawaii. Selected samples were 
tested to evaluate the physical characteristics and engineering properties of the soils 
encountered. The laboratory testing program was assigned by Bechtel National, Inc. 
The laboratory tests performed included determination of natural moisture content, dry 
density, Atterberg Limits, sieve analysis and hydrometer, specific gravity, unconfined 
compression (soil samples and rock cores), triaxial compression, consolidation, Modified Proctor, 
and corrosion tests. Results of the tests performed are presented on Plates C-1 through C-38. 
Detailed descriptions of the various tests performed for this exploration are presented below. 
Moisture and Density 
Moisture Content and Unit Weight tests were performed on selected soil samples to aid 
in defining the interface between the various soil layers encountered and correlating the layers 
between test borings. The moisture content and unit weight tests were performed in general 
accordance with ASTM D2216 and ASTM D7263, respectively. The test results are presented on 
the Logs of Borings at the appropriate sample depths. 
Atterberg Limits 
Thirty-five Atterberg Limits tests were performed on selected soil samples to evaluate the 
liquid limit (LL), plastic limit (PL), and plasticity index (PI), as an aid in classifying the soils. The 
Atterberg Limits tests were performed in general accordance with ASTM D4318 (Method A). The 
test results are summarized on the Logs of Borings at the appropriate sample depths. Graphic 
presentations of the test results are provided on Plates C-1 through C-4. 
Sieve Analysis 
To provide information on the particle size distribution of the soils encountered, 
42 Sieve Analysis tests were performed to evaluate the gradation characteristics and to aid in soil 
classification. The tests were performed in accordance with ASTM D6913. Graphic presentations 
of the test results are provided on Plates C-5 through C-13. In addition, 25 tests were conducted 
on selected soil samples to evaluate the amount of fines content. The tests were performed in 
accordance with ASTM D1140. The amount of fines content from all the gradation tests are 
summarized on Plates C-14 through C-16. 
Specific Gravity 
Six Specific gravity tests were performed on selected soil samples and rock cores in 
general accordance with ASTM D854 and ASTM C127, respectively. The test results are presented 
on Plate C-17. 
Appendix C 
Laboratory Tests 
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page C-2 
Hawaii  California 
Unconfined Compression – Soil Samples 
To evaluate the unconfined compressive strength of the on-site clayey soils, 
eight Unconfined Compression tests were performed on selected in-situ samples in accordance 
with ASTM D2166. The test results are shown on the Logs of Borings at the appropriate sample 
depths. Individual stress-strain curves of the unconfined compression tests are presented on 
Plates C-18 through C-25. 
Uniaxial Compressive Strength – Rock Cores 
Five Uniaxial Compressive Strength tests were performed on selected rock cores to 
evaluate their unconfined compressive strength of the rock formation in general accordance with 
ASTM D7012 Method C. Uniaxial compressive strength test results for the selected rock cores are 
presented on the Logs of Borings at the appropriate sample depths and presented on Plate C-26. 
Consolidated-Undrained Triaxial Compression 
To 
evaluate 
the 
long-term 
shear 
strengths 
of 
the 
clayey 
soils, 
three 
Consolidated-Undrained Triaxial Compression tests were performed on selected relatively 
undisturbed soil samples in accordance with ASTM D4767. The applied confining pressures were 
provided by Bechtel National, Inc. The test results and stress-strain curves are presented on 
Plates C-27.1 through C-29.3. 
Unconsolidated-Undrained Triaxial Compression 
To evaluate the undrained shear strength of the clayey soils, five Unconsolidated 
Undrained Triaxial Compression (TXUU) tests were performed on selected relatively undisturbed 
soil samples in accordance with ASTM D2850. The applied confining pressures for the relatively 
undisturbed samples were provided by Bechtel National, Inc. The test results and the stress-strain 
curves are presented on Plates C-30 through C-34. 
Consolidation 
One Consolidation test was performed on a selected soil sample to evaluate the 
compressibility characteristics of the on-site soils. The test was performed in accordance with 
ASTM D2435, and the test results are presented on Plates C-35.1 through C-35.18. 
Modified Proctor 
Two Modified Proctor compaction tests (ASTM D1557 C) were performed on bulk samples 
of the near-surface soils to evaluate the dry density and moisture content relationships. The test 
results are presented on Plates C-36 and C-37. 
Chemical Analyses and Soil Resistivity 
Eighteen pH (ASTM G51), four minimum resistivity (ASTM G57), and 18 chloride (EPA 300) 
and sulfate (EPA 300) content tests were performed by our office or Eurofins TestAmerica on 
Appendix C 
Laboratory Tests

     Page C-3 
Hawaii  California 
selected soil samples obtained from our field exploration. The test results are presented on 
Plate C-38.

APPENDIX E  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page E-1 
Hawaii • California 
A P P E N D I X   E 
Hammer Efficiency Calibration 
Standard penetration test energy measurement calibrations were performed for the drill 
rigs and sampling hammers use on the project. The drill rigs used for this project consisted of 
CME-75DR and CME-75DG2. The drill rigs were equipped with automatic-trip safety hammers. 
The energy delivered to the sampling rods for the various drill rig and hammer combinations 
were measured to determine the appropriate energy correction factor for the drill rig and 
hammer system. The dynamic testing was performed by Geolabs personnel in general 
accordance with ASTM D4633, “Standard Test Method for Energy Measurement for Dynamic 
Penetrometers.”  
Instrumentation 
For all the recorded tests, the instrumentation consisted of a 24-inch long AWJ rod 
subsection (1.75 OD/1.25 ID). The rod was equipped with two full-bridge foil resistance strain 
gages. Two piezo-resistive accelerometer transducers also were attached to the instrumented 
subsection. An SPT Analyzer Model SPT-8, manufactured by Pile Dynamics, Inc., was used to 
collect the data. The calibration and compliance certificates for the SPT Analyzer are presented 
in Figures E-1 and E-2 for information purposes only.  The SPT Analyzer is a computer fitted with 
a data acquisition and signal conditioning system. During driving of the drill string, the strain and 
acceleration signals are recorded and processed for each hammer blow. The strain signal is 
converted to a force record, and the acceleration signal is converted to a velocity record. The SPT 
Analyzer saves selected hammer blows containing this information to disk and determines the 
compressive stresses, displacement, and energy at the point of measurement. This information 
can be viewed on the computer screen during driving. 
Energy Measurement 
The primary purpose of the testing was to determine the energy transferred from the 
hammer to the instrumented subsection. The SPT Analyzer measurements of force and velocity 
were analyzed to calculate the energy using the FV method (EFV), which is based on both force 
and velocity measurements as follows:  
EFV = ∫ F(t) v(t) dt 
Another method, known as the EF2 method, was not used due to the limitations 
associated with the measurements (i.e., based on force alone assuming proportionality at the 
gauge locations because velocity is not used in the analysis). The EF2 method is not the 
recommended method in ASTM D4633. 
Appendix E 
Hammer Efficiency Calibration 
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page E-2 
Hawaii • California 
The rated energy (designated as ER) for the hammer is 0.35 kip-feet based on the 2.5 feet 
(30 inch) drop height and 0.14 kip (140 pound) ram. The actual efficiency or energy transfer ratio 
(ETR) for the hammer system can then be determined using EFV and ER as follows:  
ETR = (EFV/ER)*100% 
For SPT, the typical ETR is taken as 60%, based on history and experience from prior 
studies. To adjust the measured SPT N values (Nmeasured) for actual hammer performance, the ETR 
can be used to correct the Nmeasured to N60 by using the correction factor (CN) determined as 
follows: 
CN = ETR/60% 
and N60 = CN * Nmeasured 
This is a correction for energy only. The application of additional corrections for 
overburden, non-conventional samplers, etc., was not considered in this study and should be 
evaluated for appropriateness by the geotechnical engineer for this project. 
Results 
The results of the testing program are presented in Plates E-1.1 through E-1.11 for drill rig 
CME-75DR and in Plates E-2.1 through E-2.7 for drill rig CME-75DG2. The results include FMX 
(maximum measured force in the rod subsection), VMX (maximum measured velocity in the rod 
subsection), BPM (blows per minute), EFV, and ETR. The blow count, including the SPT N-value, 
and the soil description were recorded by Geolabs personnel. 
The data was presented with statistical information, including the average, maximum, 
minimum and standard deviation for the blows associated with the group of data sets (sample 
depths) for the hammer. In addition, the data for all depths for a particular hammer combined 
are also presented in the tables.  
Note that the total blows may not match the number of blows analyzed due to differences 
between the blow count observations and the PDA recorded number. In some cases, bad blows 
or unreliable data from the PDA may have been omitted.  
The following table summarizes the hammer efficiency calibration results of the tests 
conducted for the two drill rigs (CME-75DR and CME-75DG2) used for this project.  
Appendix E

APPENDIX F  
W.O. 8058-00 
GEOLABS, INC. 
     Page F-1 
Hawaii • California 
A P P E N D I X   F 
Modified California Sampler and Standard Penetration Test Sampler Blow Count Correlation 
Blow count correlation between the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) split-spoon sampler 
and the Modified California split barrel sampler was developed with side-by-side boreholes using 
both samplers at the same depths.  The following table summarizes the samplers used in the 
side-by-side correlation. 
SAMPLERS 
Sampler Type 
Outer Diameter 
(inches) 
Inner Diameter 
(inches) 
Standard Penetration Test 
(SPT) Sampler  
2.0 
1.375 
Modified California Sampler 
(MCS) 
3.0 
2.42 
(fitted with internal rings or 
brass liners) 
The SPT results typically are reported as the N-value, which is defined as the number of 
blows required to drive a standard split-spoon sampler 12 inches with a 140-pound hammer after 
6 inches of initial seating. The penetration resistance of the larger diameter modified California 
sampler is also recorded as blows per 12 inches and can be approximately related to the 
SPT N-value through correlation to determine an equivalent SPT N-value. 
The SPT N-values used for this comparison were corrected based on the efficiency of the 
hammer-rod energy transfer for the various drill rigs used to drive each sampler and are 
presented as N60 values on the SPT-MCS Blow Count Correlation Table, Table 1. A total of forty 
(40) driven samplers from our other projects were used to develop the side-by-side correlation. 
The driven samplers were obtained in various deposits consisting of coral detritus, recent and 
older alluvium, estuarine and lagoonal deposits, and beach deposits. The data used for the 
side-by-side correlation is presented on the Modified California Sampler Versus Standard 
Penetration Test Sampler Correlation Table, Table F-1. 
Correlation between the SPT and MCS drive samplers was developed by comparing 
N60 values obtained from each sampler at the same depth in side-by-side boreholes. The 
side-by-side boreholes were located a maximum distance of about 15 feet apart. In addition, N60 
values from the SPT and MCS samplers were compared within the same borehole at depths no 
greater than 5 feet and in the same material. Each SPT-MCS comparison was carefully selected 
to ensure uniformity of soil consistency and stratigraphy between each sampling event. 
Based on the results of the side-by-side comparison, a regression analysis of the 
presented data suggest that the equivalent SPT N-values would be about 56 percent of those 
recorded with the Modified California Sampler. A data plot with the regression analysis plotting 
Appendix F 
Modified California Sampler and Standard Penetration Test Sampler Blow Count Correlation

Test Sampler Correlation Plot, Plate F-1.

Submittal Instructions 
The following are the items that should be in any review report, in the order noted. 
1. List of regulatory agencies and regulations applicable to the project. Include Names and contact information
(phone numbers) for all agencies involved for follow up.
2. Contact information: generator, type of contamination, and site history in narrative form.
3. Consultant information (i.e. Names, phone numbers) include the consultant that did the original investigation
and subsequent investigations.
4. Report format for technical information.
A. Background information for site and processes.
B. Summary of investigative action. Including sampling and testing information pertinent to disposal.
C. Summary of remedial actions and how material being disposed was generated.
D. Rational for the determination that material is solid waste this should be based on applicable regulations.
E. Site location maps and site drawings.
F. Summary table of test data.
G. Laboratory data.

HDOH EALs for sites where groundwater IS NOT a current or potential source of drinking water and the site is less than 150 meters to a surface water body (HDOH Fall 2017).
ND = The sample is not detected above the laboratory detection limit
ND = The sample specific detection limit does not support the regulatory requirement
mg/kg = milligrams per kilogram
D = The reported value is from a dilution.
J = Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
J1 = Estimated: The quantitation is an estimation due to discrepancies in meeting certain analyte-specific quality control criteria
M = Manual integrated compound
Q = One or more quality control criteria failed.
NS = No Standard
Analytes with NS and ND are not presented.  If all analytes in a Method were ND below the HDOH EAL, the DLs are not presented.
Sample ID = E2 Project Number 200035, Dum Number, Boring Number, DD5 = Drydock 5
q = Estimated maximum possible concentration, quantitated using the theoretical ion ratio. The measured ion ratio does not meet qualitative ID criteria and indicates a possible interference.

HDOH EALs for sites where groundwater IS NOT a current or potential source of drinking water and the site is less than 150 meters to a surface water body (HDOH Fall 2017).
Red font indicates HDOH EAL exceedance.
ND = The sample is not detected above the laboratory detection limit
ND = The sample specific detection limit does not support the regulatory requirement
ug/L = micrograms per liter
B = Blank contamination: The analyte was detected above one-half the reporting limit in an associated blank.
D = The reported value is from a dilution.
J = Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
J1 = Estimated: The quantitation is an estimation due to discrepancies in meeting certain analyte-specific quality control criteria
H = Sample was prepped or analyzed beyond the specified holding time
M = Manual integrated compound
NA = Not Analyzed, NS = No Standard
Analytes with NS and ND are not presented.  If all analytes in a Method were ND below the HDOH EAL, the DLs are not presented.
Sample ID = E2 Project Number 200035, Dum Number, Boring Number, DD5 = Drydock 5
q = The reported result is the estimated maximum possible concentration, quantitated using the theoretical ion ratio. The measured ion ratio does not meet qualitative ID criteria and indicates a possible interference.

GC Semi VOA
Qualifier Description
4
MS, MSD: The analyte present in the original sample is greater than 4 times the matrix spike concentration; therefore, control limits are not 
applicable.
Qualifier
D
The reported value is from a dilution.
D

Job Narrative 
320-61910-1 
Revision 8-18-2020:  This report has been revised to correct sample IDs. 
Revision 8-10/2020:  This report has been revised to add TEQ calculation, and report an additional metal analyte. 
Receipt  
The samples were received on 6/18/2020 9:50 AM; the samples arrived in good condition, properly preserved and, 
where required, on ice.  The temperatures of the 6 coolers at receipt time were 0.8º C, 0.8º C, 1.2º C, 1.6º C, 1.6º C 
and 2.9º C. 
Receipt Exceptions 
The following samples were received with insufficient preservation: 200035-D6-DD55-W (320-61910-5) and 200035-
D9-DD53-W (320-61910-8).  Sample#5 HN03 container received with a pH of 3, and Sample#8 HN03 received with a 
pH of 4.  1ml of acidity added for a pH of 0-2.  Lot 0000250832 Expires 01/11/2025. 
One or more containers for the following sample was received broken or leaking: 200035-D6-DD55-W (320-61910-5). 
Sample#5 1 of 8 1liter amber received with crack lid.  Lid was taped in lab.  No volume lost. 
The container label for the following samples did not match the information listed on the Chain-of-Custody (COC): 
200035-D7-DD55-W (320-61910-7) and 200035-D9-DD53-W (320-61910-8).  Both samples#7 and 8 lists to receive

Method 7471B: The matrix spike / matrix spike duplicate (MS/MSD) recoveries for preparation batch 320-387784 and 
320-392961 and analytical batch 320-393291 were outside control limits.  Non-homogeneity in the parent sample is 
suspected because the associated laboratory control sample (LCS) recovery was within acceptance limits. 
Methods 7471A, 7471B: Due to a program malfunction for madgetech 4, a temperature data log was unable to be 
saved. Analyst observed temperature at 121 +/- 3 degrees Celsius at 15 psi for 15 minutes. No averse affect on data.

Definitions/Glossary
Job ID: 320-62314-1
Client: Element Environmental, LLC
Project/Site: #200035-PHNSY Dry Dock and DDPH
Qualifiers
GC/MS VOA
Qualifier Description
H
Sample was prepped or analyzed beyond the specified holding time
Qualifier
J
Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
M
Manual integrated compound.
Q
One or more quality control criteria failed.
U
Undetected at the Limit of Detection.
GC/MS Semi VOA
Qualifier Description
J
Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
Qualifier
M
Manual integrated compound.
Q
One or more quality control criteria failed.
U
Undetected at the Limit of Detection.
GC Semi VOA
Qualifier Description
J
Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
Qualifier
M
Manual integrated compound.
Q
One or more quality control criteria failed.
U
Undetected at the Limit of Detection.
Dioxin
Qualifier Description
J
Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
Qualifier
M
Manual integrated compound.
q
The reported result is the estimated maximum possible concentration of this analyte, quantitated using the theoretical ion ratio. The 
measured ion ratio does not meet qualitative identification criteria and indicates a possible interference.
U
Undetected at the Limit of Detection.
Metals
Qualifier Description
J
Estimated: The analyte was positively identified; the quantitation is an estimation
Qualifier
J1
Estimated: The quantitation is an estimation due to discrepancies in meeting certain analyte-specific quality control criteria.
U
Undetected at the Limit of Detection.
Glossary
These commonly used abbreviations may or may not be present in this report.
Listed under the "D" column to designate that the result is reported on a dry weight basis
Abbreviation
%R
Percent Recovery
CFL
Contains Free Liquid
CFU
Colony Forming Unit
CNF
Contains No Free Liquid
DER
Duplicate Error Ratio (normalized absolute difference)
Dil Fac
Dilution Factor
DL
Detection Limit (DoD/DOE)
DL, RA, RE, IN
Indicates a Dilution, Re-analysis, Re-extraction, or additional Initial metals/anion analysis of the sample
DLC
Decision Level Concentration (Radiochemistry)
EDL
Estimated Detection Limit (Dioxin)
LOD
Limit of Detection (DoD/DOE)
LOQ
Limit of Quantitation (DoD/DOE)
MCL
EPA recommended "Maximum Contaminant Level"
MDA
Minimum Detectable Activity (Radiochemistry)
MDC
Minimum Detectable Concentration (Radiochemistry)
MDL
Method Detection Limit
ML
Minimum Level (Dioxin)
MPN
Most Probable Number

Case Narrative
Client: Element Environmental, LLC
Job ID: 320-62314-1
Project/Site: #200035-PHNSY Dry Dock and DDPH
Job ID: 320-62314-1
Laboratory: Eurofins TestAmerica, Sacramento
Narrative
Job Narrative
320-62314-1
Revsion:  This report has been revised to include TEQ for dioxins.
Receipt 
The sample was received on 7/1/2020 12:05 PM; the sample arrived in good condition, and where required, properly preserved and on ice.  
The temperature of the cooler at receipt was 3.0º C.
Receipt Exceptions
The container label for the following sample did not match the information listed on the Chain-of-Custody (COC): 200035-D2-DD56-W 
(320-62314-1).  The container labels list date at 6/29/20 while COC lists date at 6/16/20.  Sample was logged in according to container 
label date of 6/29/20 and confirmed by client.
GC/MS VOA 
Method 8260/CALUFT DOD: Insufficient sample volume was available to perform a matrix spike/matrix spike duplicate (MS/MSD) 
associated with analytical batch 320-394370.
Method 8260C DOD: The method blank for analytical batch 320-394374 contained Naphthalene and n-Butylbenzene above the method 
detection limit.  This target analyte concentration was less than half the reporting limit (1/2RL); therefore, re-analysis of samples was not 
performed.
Method 8260C DOD: Insufficient sample volume was available to perform a matrix spike/matrix spike duplicate (MS/MSD) associated with 
analytical batch 320-394374.
Method 8260C DOD: Insufficient sample volume was available to perform a matrix spike/matrix spike duplicate  (MS/MSD) associated with 
analytical batch 320-394561.
Method 8260C DOD: Reanalysis of the following sample was performed outside of the analytical holding time due to %D low in CCV   : 
200035-D2-DD56-W (320-62314-1).
Method 8260C DOD: The continuing calibration verification  (CCV) for analytical batch 320-394374 recovered outside control limits for the 
following analytes: 1,1,2-Trichloro-1,2,2-trifluoroethane;  1,2,3-Trichlorobenzene; and Hexachlorobutadiene.  The associated sample was 
re-analyzed outside holding time.  Both sets of data have been reported. 200035-D2-DD56-W (320-62314-1) and (CCV 320-394374/3)
No additional analytical or quality issues were noted, other than those described above or in the Definitions/Glossary page.
GC/MS Semi VOA 
Method 8270D: The method blank for preparation batch 320-392307 and analytical batch 320-392639 contained Butyl benzyl phthalate 
above the method detection limit.  This target analyte concentration was less than half the reporting limit (1/2RL); therefore, re-extraction 
and/or re-analysis of samples was not performed.
Method 8270D: The RPD of the laboratory control sample (LCS) and laboratory control sample duplicate (LCSD) for preparation batch 
320-392307 and analytical batch 320-392639 recovered outside control limits for the following analytes: Benzoic acid.  The percent 
recoveries are within control limits in both LCS and LCSD.  The data has been reported as-is.
No additional analytical or quality issues were noted, other than those described above or in the Definitions/Glossary page.
GC Semi VOA 
Methods 8151A, 8151A DOD: The continuing calibration verification (CCV) associated with batch preparation batch 280-501154 and 
analytical batch 280-502008  recovered outside acceptance criteria, low biased, for see list below.  Since the associated samples were 
non-detect for this analyte, the data have been reported from the in control column. 8151  200035-D2-DD56-W (320-62314-1)

limits: Selenium (76%).  The associated sample is (320-62314-U-1-A PDS).
